summary:
So `list_products()` returns `list(self._products.values())`. This returns a list of the actual product dictionaries stored in `self._products`. When `update_product` modifies a product dictionary in place, it modifies the same dictionary that's in the values of `self._products`. So if that same dictionary is already in the cache list, the cache will see the update!

Actually wait, that means if we cache the list returned by `store.list_products()`, and then later update a product in the store, the cached list would have the updated product (since it contains references to the same dictionaries). But my test showed this wasn't happening... Oh, because in my debug test I used `copy.deepcopy`! The server doesn't do that. Let me test without deepcopy:







<function_calls>
<invoke name="Write">
<parameter name="content" string="true">#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, 'app')

from server import cache, store
import json
import time

print("Testing actual server behavior...")

# Clear cache
cache.clear()

# Get products directly from store (what server does)
products = store.list_products()
first_id = products[0]['id']
print(f"First product ID: {first_id}")
print(f"Initial product name: {products[0]['name']}")

# Set the list in cache WITHOUT deepcopy (what server does)
list_cache_key = 'products:list'
cache.set(list_cache_key, products)
print(f"\n1. Set list cache with products list (no deepcopy)")

# Update the product in store
print(f"2. Updating product name to 'UPDATED-NAME-XYZ'")
updated_product = store.update_product(first_id, {'name': 'UPDATED-NAME-XYZ'})

# Check cached list
cached_list = cache.get(list_cache_key)
if cached_list:
    # Find the same product in cached list
    for p in cached_list:
        if p['id'] == first_id:
            print(f"\n3. Product name in CACHED list: {p['name']}")
            print(f"   Store has: {updated_product['name']}")
            if p['name'] != updated_product['name']:
                print(f"   *** MISMATCH: List cache has stale data! ***")
            else:
                print(f"   *** They're the same! Cache has updated data because of shared references ***")
                
# Test if they're the same object
print(f"\n4. Checking object references...")
print(f"   products[0] is updated_product: {products[0] is updated_product}")
print(f"   cached_list[0] is updated_product: {cached_list[0] is updated_product if cached_list else 'N/A'}")

# Now test what happens with JSON serialization/deserialization
print(f"\n5. Testing JSON serialization (what happens over HTTP)...")
# When server returns JSON, it serializes the object
# When client receives it, it's a new object
import json
json_str = json.dumps(products)
decoded_list = json.loads(json_str)
print(f"   After JSON round-trip, decoded_list[0]['name']: {decoded_list[0]['name']}")

# Update product again
store.update_product(first_id, {'name': 'ANOTHER-NAME'})
print(f"\n6. Updated product name to 'ANOTHER-NAME'")
print(f"   products[0]['name'] after update: {products[0]['name']}")
print(f"   decoded_list[0]['name'] after update: {decoded_list[0]['name']}")
print(f"   Note: decoded_list has OLD name because it was deserialized from JSON")</parameter>
<parameter name="file_path" string="true">/app/test_actual_behavior.py</parameter>
</invoke>
